symptoms.symptom_name: Known severe peanut allergy with accidental ingestion

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Confirmed allergen exposure with rapid symptom onset.
Upper airway involvement is a defining feature of severe anaphylaxis.
Confirms ongoing severe reaction requiring further adrenaline dosing.
Reflects combined cutaneous and circulatory involvement typical of systemic anaphylaxis.

symptoms.symptom_name: Hypotension (BP 90/55) and audible wheeze/stridor on examination

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Objective confirmation of anaphylactic shock; clinician-only findings sealed from telehealth chat.

symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: Known allergen exposure with airway, cutaneous, and circulatory symptoms together confirm anaphylaxis requiring urgent repeat adrenaline and emergency transport.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Immediate second dose of intramuscular adrenaline via autoinjector
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Standard first-aid management for ongoing/worsening anaphylaxis after an ineffective first dose.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Paramedic assessment and transport to Emergency Department for extended observation
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Required given risk of biphasic reaction and further airway compromise.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Delayed self-directed follow-up with allergist in a few weeks without emergency care today
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Would dangerously delay life-saving emergency treatment needed now.
